B. Denoising Diffusion Models in Pedestrian
T rajectory Prediction
Denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) is a class
of deep generative models that draw inspiration from the
principles of nonequilibrium thermodynamics within the realm
of physics [22]. Conceptually, it simulates the diffusion pro-
cess like that found in nonequilibrium statistical physics;
subsequently, it leverages denoising models to reconstruct data
distributions. Notably, DDPM has demonstrated remarkable
efficacy in various generative tasks, including but not limited
to image synthesis, point cloud generation, and audio synthe-
sis [23]. Furthermore, there have been endeavors to extend
the utility of DDPM to the domain of trajectory prediction.
MID [24] pioneered the utilization of diffusion models to
model temporal variations in predictive uncertainty of trajec-
tory forecasts. Nevertheless, given the considerable temporal
overhead associated with conventional diffusion models, which
involve an extensive number of diffusion steps, the LED [25]
methodology advances the proposal to use denoising diffusion
implicit models (DDIM) [26] as a means to expedite the
sampling process. By doing so, this approach seeks to curtail
temporal expenses and enhance overall computational effi-
ciency. Nonetheless, all the techniques above have primarily
been used for predicting pedestrian trajectories within typical
scenarios and have not been specifically further refined to
address the distinctive requirements associated with semi-open
environments.
C. Pedestrian T rajectory Prediction Dataset in
Semi-Open Environments
